class_schedule.topic: Evaluating Emerging Technologies: Hype vs. Evidence
learning_objectives: Apply the Gartner Hype Cycle to assess emerging educational technologies
Distinguish between technology potential and current educational evidence
Develop a personal framework for evaluating new technologies
readings: Amara, R. - Amara's Law and technology forecasting

lecture_notes.introduction: Every year brings new technologies proclaimed to 'transform education.' Most do not. This course equips you to evaluate emerging technologies with evidence-based skepticism and identify the ones that genuinely merit attention.
key_concepts: Gartner Hype Cycle: innovation trigger, peak of inflated expectations, trough of disillusionment, slope of enlightenment, plateau of productivity
Amara's Law: we overestimate technology in the short run and underestimate it in the long run
Horizon Report methodology and limitations
Technology readiness levels for education
The 'no significant difference' phenomenon revisited
lecture_notes.demonstration: Hype Cycle placement exercise: place 10 current educational technologies on the Hype Cycle and defend each placement with evidence

review.value: We tend to overestimate the effect of a technology in the short run and underestimate it in the long run. The internet in 1995 was overhyped; the internet in 2020 exceeded most predictions. Apply this to current AI hype.
A technology may be technically mature but educationally immature. VR hardware is ready; VR pedagogy is still emerging. Evaluate both dimensions before adoption.

class_schedule.topic: Generative AI and Large Language Models in Education
learning_objectives: Evaluate current and near-future capabilities of generative AI for education
Design learning activities that appropriately integrate generative AI
Address academic integrity challenges posed by AI content generation
readings: Mollick, E. & Mollick, L. - Assigning AI: Seven Approaches for Students
UNESCO - Guidance for Generative AI in Education and Research

lecture_notes.introduction: Generative AI is the most disruptive emerging technology in education since the internet. It can write essays, solve problems, generate code, create images, and simulate conversation. The question is not whether students will use it but how we design education to be meaningful in its presence.
key_concepts: Current capabilities: text generation, code, images, multimodal understanding
Near-future capabilities: personalized tutoring, real-time translation, adaptive content generation
Seven approaches: AI as tutor, coach, mentor, teammate, student, simulator, tool
Redesigning assessment: what to assess when AI can do the product
Process-over-product assessment: documenting thinking, not just output
lecture_notes.demonstration: AI integration design exercise: redesign a traditional essay assignment for a world where students have access to generative AI, maintaining the same learning objectives
setup_requirements: Access to a generative AI tool for testing

review.value: When AI can produce the product, assess the process: require annotated drafts, thinking-aloud recordings, in-class writing, oral defense of written work, or meta-cognitive reflection on how AI was used.

Have students teach a concept to an AI, then evaluate the AI's response for errors. This requires deep understanding: you cannot evaluate an AI's explanation of photosynthesis unless you understand photosynthesis yourself.

class_schedule.topic: Blockchain, Micro-Credentials, and the Future of Credentialing
learning_objectives: Explain how blockchain technology can be applied to educational credentialing
Evaluate the promise and limitations of digital badges and micro-credentials
Design competency-based credentialing systems using emerging technologies
readings: Grech, A. & Camilleri, A. - Blockchain in Education (EU report)

lecture_notes.introduction: The traditional degree is being unbundled. Employers increasingly want verified evidence of specific skills, not just a diploma. Blockchain, digital badges, and micro-credentials are emerging as technologies for portable, verifiable skill recognition.
key_concepts: Blockchain basics: distributed ledger, immutability, smart contracts
Educational applications: verifiable credentials, transcript portability, credential fraud prevention
Digital badges: Open Badges standard, issuing, earning, and displaying
Micro-credentials: stackable, competency-based, industry-aligned
Skills-based hiring and the unbundling of the degree
lecture_notes.demonstration: Badge system design: design a micro-credential pathway for a specific skill domain, including competency definitions, assessment criteria, and badge hierarchy

review.value: Blockchain is a solution looking for a problem in most educational contexts. Existing digital signature and database technologies can provide verifiable credentials more efficiently. Blockchain adds value only when decentralized trust is genuinely needed.

Micro-credentials become powerful when they stack into recognized pathways: individual badges for data cleaning, visualization, and statistical analysis that combine into a 'Data Analytics' credential recognized by employers.

class_schedule.topic: Internet of Things and Wearables in Learning
learning_objectives: Identify educational applications of IoT sensors and wearable devices
Design data collection and learning activities using IoT
Evaluate privacy implications of sensor-rich learning environments
readings: Gartner - IoT in Education use cases
Klopfer, E. et al. - Augmented Learning with Sensor-Rich Mobile Devices

lecture_notes.introduction: The Internet of Things places computing into physical objects: sensors that measure temperature, motion, location, heart rate, and environmental conditions. In education, IoT enables authentic, data-driven learning experiences that connect digital and physical worlds.
key_concepts: Educational IoT: environmental sensors, wearable fitness trackers, smart lab equipment
Citizen science: students collecting real environmental data with IoT sensors
Biometric data in learning: attention tracking, stress detection, physiological engagement
Privacy concerns: continuous data collection on students' bodies and behaviors
Maker education: prototyping IoT devices with Arduino and Raspberry Pi
lecture_notes.demonstration: IoT learning activity design: design a science lesson where students use smartphone sensors (accelerometer, light sensor, GPS) to collect and analyze real-world data

review.value: Students using IoT sensors to collect real environmental data (air quality, water temperature, noise levels) contribute to actual scientific research while learning data collection, analysis, and scientific method.

Wearable devices that track student attention, stress, or engagement cross significant privacy boundaries. The educational value must clearly outweigh the surveillance cost, and student consent must be genuine, not coerced.

class_schedule.topic: Immersive and Spatial Computing: The Next Platform
learning_objectives: Evaluate spatial computing (Apple Vision Pro, Meta Quest) as a learning platform
Design educational experiences for mixed reality environments
Assess the readiness of spatial computing for educational deployment
readings: Bailenson, J. - VR as the Ultimate Empathy Machine (updated perspective)

lecture_notes.introduction: Spatial computing merges the digital and physical worlds seamlessly. Unlike VR (which replaces reality) or AR (which overlays reality), spatial computing creates a new computing paradigm where digital objects coexist naturally in physical space.
key_concepts: Spatial computing: MR headsets that blend digital and physical seamlessly
Educational potential: 3D anatomy, architectural walkthroughs, collaborative spatial design
Eye and hand tracking as natural input methods
Current limitations: cost, weight, battery life, social isolation
Accessibility and equity: who can afford this platform?
lecture_notes.demonstration: Spatial learning concept: design a learning experience for spatial computing (mixed reality) that could not be achieved with existing technologies, applying the DICER framework
setup_requirements: Demo videos of educational spatial computing applications

review.value: VR isolates the user from the physical world. Spatial computing integrates digital objects into the physical world, enabling collaborative, context-aware learning without isolation.

Spatial computing hardware is available but expensive ($3,500+ for Apple Vision Pro). Educational content is minimal. Pedagogical research is nearly nonexistent. We are at the Innovation Trigger stage of the Hype Cycle.

class_schedule.topic: Technology Futures: Responsible Innovation in Education
learning_objectives: Apply futures thinking methods to educational technology planning
Develop an ethical framework for evaluating new educational technologies
Articulate a vision for technology-enhanced education that centers human flourishing
readings: Selwyn, N. - Should Robots Replace Teachers? Ch. 7
Jasanoff, S. - The Ethics of Invention Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: This final session steps back from individual technologies to ask: What kind of education do we want, and what role should technology play in creating it? The answer must center human flourishing, not technological capability.
key_concepts: Futures thinking: scenarios, backcasting, wind-tunneling
Responsible innovation: anticipate, reflect, engage, act (AREA framework)
Techno-solutionism: the belief that technology can solve inherently human problems
Human flourishing as the goal: technology serves education, not the reverse
Your role: the educational technologist as ethical steward of innovation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Futures workshop: develop three scenarios for education in 2035 (optimistic, dystopian, realistic), identify the technology decisions that lead to each, and present your preferred scenario with a path to achieve it

review.value: Morozov's critique: the belief that every social problem has a technological solution. Education's challenges (poverty, racism, underfunding, teacher burnout) are structural. Technology alone cannot solve them and may distract from the real solutions needed.

Responsible innovation requires: Anticipating impacts, Reflecting on purposes and motivations, Engaging stakeholders (especially those affected), and Acting responsibly based on the first three steps.

quizzes.topic: Emerging Technology Evaluation and Generative AI

questions.question: Where would you place generative AI (e.g., ChatGPT, Claude) on the Gartner Hype Cycle as of 2024? Justify your placement with evidence of both inflated expectations and genuine capability.
questions.answer: As of 2024, generative AI is likely at or just past the Peak of Inflated Expectations. Evidence of inflated expectations: claims that AI will replace teachers, make education 'personalized at scale' overnight, or make traditional assessment obsolete. Many implementations are shallow (using AI to generate quizzes) rather than transformative. Evidence of genuine capability: AI can provide instant feedback on writing at scale, generate practice problems adapted to student level, translate content across languages in real time, and serve as a Socratic dialogue partner. The technology is real and powerful, but the gap between what it can do and what people expect it to do is currently at maximum. The trough of disillusionment will come as early implementations disappoint and AI-generated content quality issues emerge.

questions.question: Explain the 'process over product' approach to assessment in the age of generative AI. Design one specific assessment that uses this approach for a college writing course.
questions.answer: Process over product shifts assessment focus from the final output (which AI can generate) to the thinking, decision-making, and learning process that produces it. The rationale: if the goal is developing critical thinking and writing ability, the process of creating text matters more than the text itself. Specific assessment: Annotated Revision Portfolio — students submit: (1) an initial handwritten brainstorm/outline created in class, (2) a first draft with margin annotations explaining their reasoning for each argument, (3) an AI-generated version of the same prompt with the student's critique identifying what the AI got right and wrong, (4) a final revision incorporating their analysis with tracked changes, and (5) a reflective essay explaining how their thinking evolved. This makes AI a learning tool rather than a shortcut.

rubric.key: Assessment genuinely measures learning despite AI availability

quizzes.topic: Blockchain, IoT, Spatial Computing, and Futures

questions.question: A university proposes requiring all students to wear EEG headbands during lectures to measure attention levels and provide real-time engagement data to instructors. Using the AREA framework for responsible innovation, evaluate this proposal.
questions.answer: Anticipate: The data could help instructors identify when attention drops and adjust their teaching. However, attention as measured by EEG is a crude proxy for learning; students may be deeply thinking while appearing 'disengaged' by EEG metrics. False negatives and positives are likely. Reflect: What is the purpose? Improving teaching or surveilling students? Continuous biometric monitoring fundamentally changes the student-instructor relationship from trust-based to surveillance-based. Engage: Were students consulted? A requirement (not voluntary) removes agency. Students with anxiety, ADHD, or other conditions may show atypical EEG patterns that are misinterpreted. Act: Reject the proposal as currently designed. The ethical costs (surveillance, privacy invasion, stigmatization, chilling effect on intellectual risk-taking) outweigh the speculative benefits. Alternative: use anonymous, aggregated polling or exit tickets to gather engagement feedback without biometric surveillance.

rubric.key: Identifies specific ethical concerns with biometric monitoring

questions.question: Explain techno-solutionism and give a specific example from educational technology where technological thinking obscured a deeper structural problem.
questions.answer: Techno-solutionism is the belief that technology can solve problems that are fundamentally social, political, or economic. It frames complex human challenges as engineering problems with technical fixes. Example: 'Closing the achievement gap with adaptive learning software.' The achievement gap between wealthy and low-income students is caused by disparities in school funding, teacher quality, neighborhood safety, nutrition, healthcare, and family stability. Deploying adaptive software without addressing these structural factors may produce marginal test score gains while leaving the fundamental inequities untouched. Worse, it may redirect funding and attention from proven interventions (smaller class sizes, experienced teachers, wrap-around services) toward technology purchases. The technology addresses a symptom while ignoring the disease.

rubric.key: Identifies the structural problem being obscured

major_assignments.title: Emerging Technology Evaluation Report and Implementation Proposal
major_assignments.description: Select an emerging technology not yet widely adopted in education. Produce: (1) a comprehensive evaluation including current capabilities, evidence base, Hype Cycle placement, and pedagogical potential; (2) an implementation proposal for a specific educational context with DICER justification, ethical impact assessment, and pilot study design; (3) a responsible innovation assessment using the AREA framework.
objectives: Evaluate an emerging technology using multiple frameworks
Design a responsible pilot implementation for an educational context
Apply ethical and equity lenses to technology adoption decisions
deliverables: Implementation proposal with pilot study design

criteria: Accurate description of current capabilities and limitations
Evidence base honestly assessed with research quality evaluation
Hype Cycle placement justified with evidence
DICER justification for why this technology is needed
Clear learning objectives and evaluation plan
Rigorous technology evaluation with evidence-based Hype Cycle placement
Feasible implementation proposal with clear rationale

quizzes.description: Bi-weekly quizzes on emerging technology evaluation and ethical frameworks

participation.description: Technology demos, futures thinking exercises, and ethical debates

major_assignments.description: Emerging technology evaluation and implementation proposal

midterm_examination.description: Covers Hype Cycle, generative AI, blockchain, IoT, and evaluation frameworks

final_project.description: Futures scenario project: comprehensive vision for technology in education 2035 with implementation roadmap

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected. Technology demonstrations and futures workshops require participation. More than 3 absences will reduce the final grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work loses 5% per day for up to 5 days.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion and debate are essential. All evaluation reports must be individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. AI tools may be used as part of exploration with disclosure. Violations result in a zero and referral.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Computer with internet access. Willingness to explore new technologies. Access to AI tools and demo accounts provided.
